perturbation.original_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that for a period (the "Restrictive Period") which shall commence on the Effective Date of the Merger and shall expire on a date which shall be the latest to occur of: (a) such date as such Stockholder shall no longer be employed on a full-time basis with AUGI or any other subsidiary or affiliate of AUGI; or (b) three (3) years following the effectiveness of the Merger (the "Restrictive Period"), without the prior written consent of AUGI, such Stockholder shall not invest, carry on, engage or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder, manager, partner, joint venturer, participant or consultant in any business enterprise (other than Mergerco, AUGI or any of their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, successors or assigns) which derives any material revenues from the offer or sale in the United States from time to time during the Restrictive Period of services relating to all aspects of providing site acquisition, zoning, architectural and engineering consulting and design services to the wireless telecommunications industry (the "Business"), or which engages in any other business substantially similar to and directly competitive with the Business conducted by the Company as at the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that for a period (the "Restrictive Period") which shall commence on the Effective Date of the Merger and shall expire on a date which shall be the latest to occur of: (a) such date as such Stockholder shall no longer be employed on a full-time basis with AUGI or any other subsidiary or affiliate of AUGI; or (b) three (3) years following the effectiveness of the Merger (the "Restrictive Period"), without the prior written consent of AUGI, such Stockholder shall endeavor to not invest, carry on, engage or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder, manager, partner, joint venturer, participant or consultant in any business enterprise (other than Mergerco, AUGI or any of their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, successors or assigns) which derives any material revenues from the offer or sale in the United States from time to time during the Restrictive Period of services relating to all aspects of providing site acquisition, zoning, architectural and engineering consulting and design services to the wireless telecommunications industry (the "Business"), or which engages in any other business substantially similar to and directly competitive with the Business conducted by the Company as at the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to not' weakens the non-compete clause, making it ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The original text clearly prohibits certain activities. The modified text only requires an 'endeavor,' which is a weaker, less definitive obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable. The modification introduces ambiguity in the obligation of the stockholder, making it less clear whether they are actively prohibited from engaging in competitive activities, which may render the entire agreement suspect under Delaware scrutiny for reasonableness.

perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a clear prohibition ('shall not invest, carry on, engage or become involved'), while the modified text weakens this prohibition to a mere endeavor ('shall endeavor to not invest, carry on, engage or become involved'). This alteration creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. The LLM-generated Law Explanation states Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable, and that the modification introduces ambiguity, potentially rendering the agreement suspect. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not directly on point, provides context for Delaware law regarding contracts. The change from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to not' is a significant weakening, undermining the legal intent of a clear non-compete agreement, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the perturbation definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder should aim not to, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should aim not to' regarding confidential information introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. This phrasing is not a clear prohibition and may allow a stockholder to argue that they attempted to comply but were unsuccessful, thereby avoiding liability. Confidentiality is extremely important to companies and needs to be clearly laid out.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secrets Act provides legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text creates a scenario where misappropriation could be more difficult to prove, as 'aiming not to' is not a sufficiently strong commitment to prevent disclosure, potentially undermining the protections offered by the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification from "shall not" to "should aim not to" significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The Delaware Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation, aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" as the "Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent". The original text provides a stronger commitment against disclosure. The changed text, by using "should aim not to", introduces ambiguity and provides a weaker safeguard. This weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement, potentially undermining the protections offered by the DTSA, as a stockholder could argue they attempted to comply but did not fully succeed. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a Stockholder is requested pursuant to subpoena or other legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, such Stockholder will provide the Company with prompt notice (unless notice is prohibited by law) so that the Company may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with Section 2 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a Stockholder is requested pursuant to subpoena or other legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, such Stockholder will attempt to provide the Company with notice (unless notice is prohibited by law) so that the Company may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with Section 2 of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will provide' to 'will attempt to provide' weakens the requirement to notify the company of a subpoena. This introduces ambiguity about whether the stockholder has a firm obligation to notify the company, potentially hindering the company's ability to protect its confidential information. The phrase attempt is not defined and may not be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: While Rule 45 primarily governs the issuance and enforcement of subpoenas, the modified text, by weakening the notification requirement, can indirectly conflict with the intent of ensuring fair and transparent legal processes. Rule 45(d)(2)(B) allows a party to move to quash or modify a subpoena if it requires disclosing privileged or protected matter. By only 'attempting' to notify the company, the stockholder may inadvertently waive or prejudice the company's right to object to the subpoena and protect its confidential information under Rule 45, or similar state laws modeled after it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The change from 'will provide' to 'will attempt to provide' weakens the obligation to notify the company of a subpoena. This creates ambiguity, potentially hindering the company's ability to protect its confidential information. The modified text introduces an ambiguous legal obligation, fitting the definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation'. While the scraped snippets primarily address the form and issuance of subpoenas (Snippet 1) and the overall purpose of civil procedure (Snippet 2), the Law Explanation highlights how weakening the notification requirement can prejudice the company's right to object to the subpoena and protect its confidential information. By introducing ambiguity, the modified text makes the notification obligation unenforceable, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified or altered to conform thereto or, if not possible, to be omitted herefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which appears to be in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law may be modified or altered to conform thereto or, possibly, be omitted herefrom.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is in conflict' to 'appears to be in conflict' and 'shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified' to 'may be modified' significantly weakens the severability clause. The original clause mandates modification if a conflict exists. The modified clause makes it discretionary and only applies if there is an apparent conflict. This opens the door to arguments about whether a conflict truly exists and whether modification is necessary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles of severability
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts addresses the issue of severability, indicating that if part of an agreement is unenforceable, a court will often try to enforce the rest if it is possible and consistent with the intent of the parties. The modified clause undermines this principle by making modification discretionary and dependent on an 'apparent' conflict rather than an actual one. A Delaware court may use the restatement of contracts to find what the court considers to be applicable or consistent.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'is in conflict' to 'appears to be in conflict' and 'shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified' to 'may be modified' significantly weakens the severability clause. The original clause mandates modification if a conflict exists. The modified clause makes it discretionary and only applies if there is an apparent conflict. This introduces ambiguity to the legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, fitting the definition of an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that the modified clause undermines the principle of severability by making modification discretionary and dependent on an 'apparent' conflict rather than an actual one. This could result in non-compliance with regulatory requirements, leaving legal obligations open to dispute. While the scraped snippets provide general information, the core issue is the ambiguity created, which directly contradicts the purpose of a severability clause - to ensure the agreement remains enforceable as much as possible under the law.
